coredata.dc:title: Regrouping strategy of retired batteries considering SOC consistency
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2022 The Author(s). Published by Elsevier Ltd.
coredata.prism:publisher: The Author(s). Published by Elsevier Ltd.
coredata.prism:issueName: 2022 The 5th International Conference on Renewable Energy and Environment Engineering
coredata.dc:creator: Liu, Xinghua
Chang, Guoyi
Tian, Jiaqiang
Li, Siqi
Chen, Chaobo
coredata.dc:description: This paper proposes a method for retired batteries based on SOC consistency matching. This method takes the SOC consistency of the battery pack as the standard, which calculates the model parameter matching relationship to optimize the performance of the pack. Based on Thevenin equivalent circuit model, the mathematical model of lithium ion battery is established and the model is parameterized. Additionally, a battery pack reconfiguration criterion based on SOC consistency is proposed to reveal the mapping relationship between cell parameters and battery pack SOC consistency. Finally, the battery parameters are identified by the recursive least squares (RLS) algorithm to select two groups of batteries. Dynamic and constant current tests are performed on battery packs with different topologies, which are used to verify the feasibility of the proposed method. The experimental results show that the proposed reorganization scheme can better ensure the consistency of the SOC of the battery pack during charging and discharging.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Retired batteries
SOC consistency
Battery regrouping
Reconfiguration criterion

No. 62203352 , U2003110 ), the Key Laboratory Project of Shaanxi Provincial Department of Education, PR China (No. 20JS110 ), Xi’an Science and Technology Planning Project, PR China (No. 2020KJRC0084 ) and High Level Talents Plan of Shaanxi Province for Young Professionals, PR China .

No. 62203352, U2003110), the Key Laboratory Project of Shaanxi Provincial Department of Education, PR China (No. 20JS110), Xi'an Science and Technology Planning Project, PR China (No. 2020KJRC0084) and High Level Talents Plan of Shaanxi Province for Young Professionals, PR China.

ce:dochead.ce:textfn: 2022 The 5th International Conference on Renewable Energy and Environment Engineering (REEE 2022), 24–26 August, 2022, Brest, France

head.ce:title: Regrouping strategy of retired batteries considering SOC consistency

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Electrical Engineering, Xi’an University of Technology, Xi’an 710048, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Electrical Engineering, Xi’an University of Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Electrical Engineering, Xi’an University of Technology, Xi’an 710048, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Electronic Information Engineering, Xi’an Technological University, Xi’an 710021, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Electronic Information Engineering, Xi’an Technological University

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Electronic Information Engineering, Xi’an Technological University, Xi’an 710021, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: This paper proposes a method for retired batteries based on SOC consistency matching. This method takes the SOC consistency of the battery pack as the standard, which calculates the model parameter matching relationship to optimize the performance of the pack. Based on Thevenin equivalent circuit model, the mathematical model of lithium ion battery is established and the model is parameterized. Additionally, a battery pack reconfiguration criterion based on SOC consistency is proposed to reveal the mapping relationship between cell parameters and battery pack SOC consistency. Finally, the battery parameters are identified by the recursive least squares (RLS) algorithm to select two groups of batteries. Dynamic and constant current tests are performed on battery packs with different topologies, which are used to verify the feasibility of the proposed method. The experimental results show that the proposed reorganization scheme can better ensure the consistency of the SOC of the battery pack during charging and discharging.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: As the core component of electric vehicles, power batteries have an average life span of 5 to 8 years. When the capacity of the power battery decays below 80% of the rated capacity, the batteries need to be retired [1,2]. Recycling the retired battery can give full play to its remaining economic value [3,4].

At present, there are two main methods for disposing of retired batteries [5]. One is the recycling of battery materials [6], but this treatment method has a high cost of recycling and is likely to cause environmental pollution problems [7]. Another method is to propose a cascade utilization plan [8,9]. For echelon utilization, the reorganization of retired batteries is a key link. The purpose of reorganization is to find a battery pack with high consistency [10]. Commonly used reorganization methods are mainly based on the sorted batteries, reorganizing batteries with similar parameters. A clustering algorithm for retired batteries based on traversal optimization is proposed in Ref. [11]. Although this method avoids iteration, it is difficult to deal with the problem of uneven data density. An improved K-means clustering algorithm is investigated in Ref. [12]. Although this method is simple and easy to implement, it lacks a strict mathematical evaluation basis, which cannot reorganize batteries with large differences in performance parameters. A pathway for repurposing RLFPPB by using echelon exploitation method was investigated in Ref. [13].

To overcome the shortcomings of the above researches, this work investigates the dynamic consistency of the recombined battery pack, aiming to provide a strict mathematical judgment basis for the reorganization of retired power batteries. The main contribution is to propose a recombination method for retired power batteries based on SOC consistency matching, which uses SOC as the consistency criterion. Compared with the traditional sorting method [14], this work addresses the unreliable performance of single-parameter cell reorganization and the need for strict consistency of cell parameters. The proposed method has significant ecological and economic benefits for the large-scale promotion and application of energy storage power stations, reducing energy storage costs, and protecting the ecological environment. And it has great practical significance and application value for the establishment of standardization of waste battery recycling system.

This article is organized as follows. Battery modeling and parameter identification are introduced in Section 2. Section 3 discusses battery reorganization rules based on SOC consistency. Section 4 is the experiment and result analysis, which verifies the consistency of the battery pack under different working conditions. Conclusions are given in Section 5.

The Thevenin model [15] consists of a voltage source, an ohmic resistance
R
0
, a polarization resistance
R
p
and a polarization capacitance
C

. According to Kirchhoff’s law, the terminal voltage
U

U

Parameter identification is the basis of battery reconfiguration design. The RLS algorithm is widely used in parameter identification due to its fast convergence and excellent robustness. The system model is regarded as a SISOS (single-input single-output system) with charging current I as input and voltage
U

Due to the different performance levels of retired batteries, it is necessary to restructure them consistently. For the battery pack, SOCs of all cells are expected to be as consistent as possible. The SOC of the battery is determined by its parameters. Therefore, the consistency of battery pack SOC can be guaranteed as much as possible through reasonable configuration parameters. In this work, a regrouping scheme is proposed based on SOC consistency criterion for the battery pack with different topologies.

It is assumed that the
SOC
0
of each battery is the same, Eq. (3) can be written as,
Δ S O C =
( I × Δ t )
/ C
, where Δ
SOC represents the change in SOC, and Δ
t denotes the charging time. The SOC consistency criterion is different for battery packs with different topologies.

The series connection of batteries is shown in Fig. 1(a). The SOC consistency of a series battery pack is decided by capacity, so the consistency between two batteries can be expressed by the following formula,
(12)
Δ S O
C
1
Δ S O
C
2
=
C
2
C
1

The parallel topology is shown in Fig. 1(b). According to Ohm’s law, the battery current is inversely proportional to its internal resistance. Coupled with formula (3), we can deduce that the consistency of a series battery pack is decided by the product of capacity and internal resistance. Since this work focuses on the consistency under constant operating conditions, the internal resistance R is expressed by the sum of ohmic resistance
R
0
and polarization resistance
R
p
. Therefore, the SOC consistency between the two batteries can be expressed by the following formula,
(13)
Δ S O
C
1
Δ S O
C
2
=
C
2
I
1
C
1
I
2
=
C
2
R
2
C
1
R
1
The battery pack with hybrid topology is more complex. For the convenience of discussion, this work takes the two-series two-parallel hybrid battery packs shown in Fig. 2 as examples for analysis.

The topology of the series connection first and then the parallel connection is shown in Fig. 2(a). For this situation, the classic method is that the battery connected in series with each branch can be regarded as an equivalent battery, then the whole circuit is the case of two batteries in parallel. However, it has an obvious defect, that is, a certain combination may be consistent in the parallel criterion, but the capacity of the two batteries on a branch is different, which leads to the overall poor consistency. In a short time Δ
t, the ratio of Δ
SOC of two batteries connected in series is expressed as,
(14)
Δ S O
C
1 , 1
Δ S O
C
1 , 2
=
C
12
C
11
where battery No. (1, 1) and battery No. (1, 2) are shown in Fig. 2(a). The current ratio of two branches is solved as,
(15)
I
1
I
2
=
R
21
+
R
22
R
11
+
R
12

Therefore, Δ
SOC of the two batteries on different branches, such as battery No. (1, 1) and battery No. (2, 2), is expressed as,
(16)
Δ S O
C
1 , 1
Δ S O
C
2 , 1
=
C
21
(
R
21
+
R
22
)
C
11
(
R

R

)

The topology of first in parallel and then in series is shown in Fig. 2(b), which has similar defects with the former. Therefore, this work conducts the following analysis. In a short time Δ
t, the ratio of Δ

(17)
Δ S O

According to Kirchhoff’s current law, the relationship between the battery current is found as follows,
(18)

(19)
Δ S O

)

For the battery series-parallel hybrid system of Fig. 3, the internal resistance of the cell is expressed as
R
i j
, and the capacity is
C
i j
, where i is an integer with a serial number and its value is [1, n], and j is an integer with a parallel number and its value is [1, m].

According to the above derivation results, in the case of series connection followed by parallel connection, for two batteries of different branches, the ratio of the Δ
SOC of battery No. (i, j) and battery No. (x, y) is expressed as,
(20)
Δ S O

Y = 1

In the case of parallel connection followed by series connection, the ratio of the Δ

(21)
Δ S O

X = 1

R
I j

In order to compare the consistency between different batteries, a measure of consistency distance must be determined. The most common distance definition method is the Euclidean distance. For example, the Euclidean distance between battery No. (i, j) and battery No. (x, y) is expressed as,
(22)
d
i j _ x y
=
( Δ S O
C
i j
− Δ S O
C
x y
)
2

Although the geometric meaning of this method is clear, it measures an absolute distance. According to the above discussion, the proportion of SOC change between batteries should be paid more attention to. However, since only the proportion of Δ
SOC is considered, two new problems have arisen. On the one hand, the closer
d
i j _ x y
is to 1, the smaller the consistency distance between the two batteries. This is inconsistent with the common sense in life that the closer the distance is to 0, the closer the two are. On the other hand, the distance from No. (i, j) to No. (x, y) is not equal to the distance from No. (x, y) to No. (i, j). Considering the method of stretching of the basic inequality, the following distance improvement scheme can be obtained,
(23)
d
i j _ x y
=
Δ S O
C
i j
Δ S O
C
x y
+
Δ S O
C
x y
Δ S O
C
i j
− 2

This distance measurement method can better represent the consistent distance between batteries. The paper names this distance measurement method symmetric proportional distance. Therefore, the inconsistency of the entire battery pack δ can be expressed as,
(24)
δ = ∑
d
i j _ x y

In this work, we established an experimental platform to verify the validity of the presented method, which includes a battery tester and a computer, as shown in Fig. 4. The battery parameters are shown in Table 1.

To verify the accuracy and effectiveness of our proposed method, we conduct capacity test, OCV test and parameter identification on the battery. In this experiment, the static capacity test was performed in the first step to measure the battery capacity, and then the battery was fully charged. In the second step, the cells were discharged at a rate of 1C for 6 min and then left to stand for 1 h and the OCV was recorded. Repeat the second step 10 times, so that it can measure the OCV from 100% to 0% SOC. The OCV test results are shown in Fig. 5(a). Furthermore, the coefficients of the OCV-SOC model can be determined by fitting, as shown in Table 2.
Further, The battery is tested with dynamic current for parameter identification. The current is shown in Fig. 6(b). The identification results are shown in Fig. 5.

At both ends of the SOC range, the parameter change trend is relatively obvious due to the polarization effect. For electric vehicles, the SOC practical range of battery pack is usually 20% ∼ 80%. In this work, the average value of parameters is determined from the SOC range of 20% ∼ 80%, as shown in Table 3.
In this work, the above method is used to identify parameters, and two groups of batteries are screened out to verify the battery consistency criteria.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Consistent recombination criterion verification for batteries
ce:section.ce:para: In this work, the battery pack is composed of two series-parallel groups. Each group is composed of 4 battery cells, and the consistency of the series and then the parallel and the first parallel and then the series are discussed separately. The regrouped battery packs are tested with different working conditions. The constant current is shown in Fig. 7(a). The dynamic current is shown in Fig. 6(b).

The cell parameters of the battery pack in series and then in parallel are shown in Table 4. The parallel connection of No. 1, No. 2 series group and No. 3, No. 4 series group is regarded as a random combination, and the battery pack inconsistency δ under this combination is 0.243. The optimal combination calculated by using the above-mentioned SOC-based consistency reorganization method is No. 1, No. 3 series group and No. 2, No. 4 series group in parallel, and the battery pack inconsistency under this combination is 0.007. According to the calculation results, this work verifies the two combination methods respectively, and the results are shown in Fig. 7.

It can be seen from the results that Fig. 7(a) is SOC curves of a randomly assembled battery pack under constant current conditions, in which the maximum SOC difference observed is 0.097. The voltage curves are shown in Fig. 7(c), where the maximum voltage difference that can be observed is 0.146 V. Fig. 7(b) is the SOC of the optimized composite battery pack under constant current condition, and it can be observed that the maximum difference in SOC is 0.002. The voltage curves of this scenario are shown in Fig. 7(d), where the maximum voltage difference that can be observed is 8.62 × 10−4 V. In addition, Fig. 7(e) is SOC curves of a randomly assembled battery pack under dynamic condition. It is observed that when the battery No. 4 is reached cut off voltage, the capacity of the battery No. 1 with the most remaining capacity is 9.5%. The voltage curves are shown in Fig. 7(g). Fig. 7(f) is SOC curves of the optimized battery pack. It can be observed that when the battery No. 4 reaches cut off voltage, the battery No. 3 with the most remaining power has only 0.2% of the capacity. The voltage curve of this scene is shown in Fig. 7(h). From the results, it is not difficult to find that after optimization and reorganization, the consistency of the battery pack has been greatly improved.

The battery cell data of the battery pack in parallel and then in series are shown in Table 5. The series connection of No. 1, No. 2 parallel group and No. 3, No. 4 parallel group is regarded as a random combination, and the battery pack inconsistency δ under this combination is 0.038. The optimal combination calculated by the above-mentioned SOC-based consistency reorganization method is the No. 1, No. 3 parallel group and the No. 2, No. 4 parallel group in series, and the battery pack inconsistency under this combination is 6.52 × 10−4. According to the calculation results, this work verifies the two combination methods respectively, and the results are shown in Fig. 8.

The result is similar to the case of series first and then parallel. Fig. 8(a) shows the SOC curve of randomly assembled battery pack under constant current conditions, in which the maximum SOC difference observed is 0.104. The voltage curves of this scene are shown in Fig. 8(c), where the maximum voltage difference that can be observed is 0.169 V. Fig. 8(b) is the SOC curves of the optimized composite battery pack, and the maximum difference in SOC can be observed to be 7.69 × 10−4. The voltage curves of this scene are shown in Fig. 8(d), where the maximum voltage difference that can be observed is 0.011 V. In addition, Fig. 8(e) is the SOC curves of a randomly assembled battery pack under dynamic conditions. It is observed that when the battery No. 4 is empty, the capacity of the battery No. 2 with the most remaining capacity is 10.8%. The voltage curves of this scene are shown in Fig. 8(g). Fig. 8(f) is the SOC curves of the optimized battery pack under dynamic conditions. It can be observed that when the battery No. 3 is empty, the battery No. 2 with the most remaining power has only 0.8% of the capacity. The voltage curves of this scene are shown in Fig. 8(h). Similarly, after optimization and reorganization, the consistency of the battery pack has been greatly improved.

In this paper, we have come up with an analysis method based on SOC consistency to regroup retired batteries. Based on series and parallel topology battery packs, the principle of battery reconfiguration and matching has been analyzed. And extended to hybrid topology battery pack, a general battery reorganization principle has been proposed. By reasonably configuring the internal resistance and capacity of the cell, it has ensured that the SOC of the battery pack tends to be consistent as much as possible. Finally, the performance of the proposed scheme has been verified under constant current and dynamic current conditions.

Since the consistent SOC can only indicate the consistent capacity of the battery under charging and discharging conditions, it cannot reflect the working characteristics of the battery well, so this method has certain limitations. Therefore, a joint consistency sorting method based on voltage and SOC will be proposed in our later work.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported in part by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant. No. 62203352, U2003110), the Key Laboratory Project of Shaanxi Provincial Department of Education, PR China (No. 20JS110), Xi’an Science and Technology Planning Project, PR China (No. 2020KJRC0084) and High Level Talents Plan of Shaanxi Province for Young Professionals, PR China .
